chapter1.title: The Story of Zero
chapter1.coreConceptDesc: The story of zero reveals the mystery of base conversion. In the computer world, all numbers are ultimately converted to 0s and 1s. Understanding binary not only helps us program better, but also gives insight into the essential structure of numbers.
chapter1.keyInsightDesc: Zero plays the crucial role of a "placeholder" in number systems. Without 0, we cannot distinguish between 1, 10, and 100. In binary, 0 and 1 are equally important — 0 means "off" and 1 means "on", which is exactly how computer circuits work.

chapter2.title: Logic
chapter2.coreConceptDesc: Logic is the foundation of mathematics and the cornerstone of computer programs. Through logic gates, we can build complex judgment and decision systems. Understanding truth tables and logical operations is a must on the path to becoming an excellent programmer.
chapter2.keyInsightDesc: Logic gates are the "neurons" of computers. By combining simple logic gates, we can build CPUs, memory, and entire computer systems. In programming, if statements, while loops, and boolean expressions are all direct applications of logic.

chapter3.title: Remainders
chapter3.coreConceptDesc: Remainder and modular arithmetic are among the most commonly used mathematical tools in programming. From calculating weekdays to hash functions, from parity checks to graph theory, remainders help us discover periodic patterns behind numbers.
chapter3.weekdayDesc: Enter the number of days to calculate which day of the week it will be N days from today. Using modular arithmetic: weekday = (today + N) % 7
chapter3.chessMagicDesc: This is a classic magic trick. The apprentice can instantly identify the flipped piece by calculating the parity bit. Click pieces to flip them and observe the parity change.
chapter3.parityDesc: Parity check = XOR sum of all black pieces. When a piece is flipped, the parity bit changes, allowing the change to be detected.
chapter3.sevenBridgesDesc: Can you cross all 7 bridges, each exactly once? Euler proved through graph theory that this is impossible. Click the region buttons below to try walking the bridges and verify for yourself why there's no solution.
chapter3.canvasHint: Click a region to start walking bridges, each bridge can only be crossed once
chapter3.clickRegion: Click a region to start walking
chapter3.keyInsightDesc: Remainder operations allow us to discover periodic patterns in numbers. In programming, modular arithmetic is widely used in circular arrays, hash tables, time calculations, random number generation, and more. Understanding remainders means understanding the cyclical nature of numbers.
chapter3.impossible: Impossible! All bridges crossed or no available bridges
chapter3.eulerDesc: Euler proved: a path that traverses each edge exactly once exists if and only if the graph has 0 or 2 odd-degree vertices. The Seven Bridges of Königsberg has 4 odd-degree vertices, so it's impossible!

chapter4.title: Mathematical Induction
chapter4.coreConceptDesc: Mathematical induction is a powerful tool for proving propositions about infinite sets. Just like knocking over dominoes, if you can prove the first case holds and that each case implies the next, you've proven it for all cases.
chapter4.errorProofDesc: The famous "all horses are the same color" false proof. Can you find where the error is?
chapter4.proofTitle2: "Proof": All horses are the same color
chapter4.proofBaseCase: Base Case: One horse is certainly the same color as itself.
chapter4.proofHypothesis: Inductive Hypothesis: Assume any k horses are the same color.
chapter4.proofStep: Inductive Step: Consider k+1 horses. Remove the first one, the remaining k are the same color; remove the last one, the remaining k are also the same color. Therefore all k+1 horses are the same color.
chapter4.errorAnalysis2: The error lies in the jump from k=1 to k=2! When k=1, removing the first horse and removing the last horse gives two disjoint sets. You cannot conclude they have the same color. The inductive step fails at k=1.
chapter4.codeComment10: // At the start of each loop iteration, result = 1 + 2 + ... + (i-1)
chapter4.keyInsightDesc: Mathematical induction is not only a proof tool but also the theoretical foundation of recursive programming. Every recursive call is an inductive step, and the termination condition is the base case. Understanding induction means being able to write correct recursive programs.
chapter4.dominoDesc: Watch dominoes fall one by one. This is the visual demonstration of mathematical induction — when the first falls, the rest follow.
chapter4.sumDesc: Verify that 1 + 2 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2. Adjust the slider and compare manual calculation with the formula.
chapter4.verifyPass: Verified! Both methods give the same result
chapter4.errorAnalysisDesc: The error lies in the jump from k=1 to k=2! When k=1, removing the first horse and removing the last horse gives two disjoint sets. You cannot conclude they have the same color. The inductive step fails at k=1.
chapter4.hypothesisDesc: Assume P(k) holds for all n.
chapter4.stepDesc: If P(k) holds, then P(k+1) also holds.

chapter5.title: Permutations & Combinations
chapter5.coreConceptDesc: Permutations and combinations are core tools for solving counting problems. From simple tree planting problems to the complex inclusion-exclusion principle, mastering these techniques helps us avoid double counting or missing cases.
chapter5.treePlantingDesc: Classic tree planting problem: if you plant a tree every {spacing}m and plant {count} trees total, how long is the road? Note: number of trees = number of gaps + 1
chapter5.inclusionExclusionDesc: |A ∪ B| = |A| + |B| - |A ∩ B|. When calculating the union of two sets, subtract the overlapping part that was counted twice.
chapter5.codeComment3: // Permutation: arrange k elements from n
chapter5.codeComment4: // Combination: select k elements from n
chapter5.keyInsightDesc: The core of permutations and combinations is "no duplicates, no omissions". Permutations care about order, combinations don't. The inclusion-exclusion principle helps handle overlapping cases, ensuring each element is counted exactly once. These concepts are widely used in probability, statistics, and algorithm analysis.
chapter5.permutationDesc: Permutation cares about order. P(n,k) = n! / (n-k)!

chapter6.title: Recursion
chapter6.coreConceptDesc: Recursion is the art of defining something in terms of itself. By breaking large problems into identical smaller ones, we can solve complex challenges with concise code. The Tower of Hanoi is a classic example for understanding recursion.
chapter6.codeComment4: // 2. Move the nth disk from 'from' to 'to'
chapter6.codeComment9: // Each additional disk doubles the moves!
chapter6.keyInsightDesc: The essence of recursion is "divide and conquer". The number of Tower of Hanoi moves grows exponentially with the number of disks (2^n-1), demonstrating both the power and potential performance pitfalls of recursion. Understanding termination conditions and recursive call relationships is the key to mastering this powerful tool.
chapter6.hanoiDesc: Solving Tower of Hanoi with {disks} disks, minimum {moves} moves needed
chapter6.fractalDesc: The Sierpinski triangle is a classic fractal. Adjust the recursion depth to see how the triangle keeps subdividing.

chapter7.title: Exponential Explosion
chapter7.paperThickness: Paper thickness ~0.1mm, doubles with each fold
chapter7.guessNumber: I'm thinking of a number between 1-100
chapter7.coreConceptDesc: Exponential explosion reveals the astonishing power of growth speed. Understanding exponential growth helps us design more efficient algorithms and avoid falling into the "combinatorial explosion" trap when solving problems.
chapter7.paperFoldingDesc: A sheet of paper is about 0.1mm thick, doubling with each fold. Click the milestones below to see how many folds it takes to go from a sheet of paper to the observable universe.
chapter7.paperInsight: After 42 folds, the thickness reaches about 440,000 km, exceeding the Earth-Moon distance (384,000 km)! After 103 folds, it surpasses the observable universe's diameter. This is the power of exponential explosion.
chapter7.logScaleDesc: On logarithmic scale, the growth trends of all three complexities are clearly visible.
chapter7.linearScaleDesc: On linear scale, O(2^n) quickly shoots off the chart — this is exactly what "exponential explosion" means. Try enabling logarithmic scale.
chapter7.guessingGameDesc: I'm thinking of a number between 1-100. Use binary search strategy: each guess can halve the range.
chapter7.codeComment3: // Worst case: check all n elements
chapter7.codeComment5: // Each iteration halves the range: n → n/2 → n/4 → ... → 1
chapter7.keyInsightDesc: Exponential growth far exceeds intuition. In algorithm design, O(2^n) complexity means that even for small inputs, the computation may be impossible to complete. Understanding complexity analysis and choosing the right algorithm is key to efficient programming.
chapter7.linearDesc: Check every element, time proportional to input
chapter7.logarithmicDesc: Eliminate half each time, time proportional to log of input

chapter8.title: Unsolvable Problems
chapter8.undecidable: Undecidable — This is the Halting Problem
chapter8.collatzDesc: For any positive integer n: even→n/2, odd→3n+1, does it always reach 1?
chapter8.coreConceptDesc: Not all problems have algorithmic solutions. The Halting Problem proves that no program can determine whether any arbitrary program will halt — this is the most important unsolvable problem in computer science.
chapter8.haltingDesc: Select example code and click "Analyze Program" to see if the system can determine whether the program will terminate.
chapter8.collatzWarning: Did not reach 1 within 500 steps — but this doesn't mean it never stops, we just don't know
chapter8.diagonalDesc: Suppose all real numbers are listed (one per row). Look at the diagonal (row 1 digit 1, row 2 digit 2...), add 1 to each digit to construct a new number. The new number differs from row i at position i → cannot equal any row → "listing all reals" is impossible → reals are uncountable.
chapter8.diagonalExplanation: Each digit differs from the corresponding row → not in any list
chapter8.tmDesc: Turing Machine = infinite tape + read/write head + state transition rules. It's the theoretical model for all computable problems.
chapter8.tmBinaryDesc: Binary Incrementer — scans right to the end, then carries from right to left (1→0 continue, 0→1 stop), completing binary +1
chapter8.tmZeroOneDesc: 0^n 1^n Detector — determines if input is "n zeros followed by n ones" (e.g., 0011, 000111). The Turing machine scans back and forth, pairing off 0s and 1s.
chapter8.tmBeaverDesc: Busy Beaver — writes the most 1s and halts within 2 states. If we knew the Busy Beaver's step limit, we could determine if any program halts — but the Busy Beaver itself is uncomputable!
chapter8.keyInsightDesc: The Halting Problem reveals the inherent limits of computation. It's not a technical limitation, but a mathematical impossibility.
chapter8.collatzReach: For verified numbers, the Collatz conjecture always reaches 1
chapter8.collatzUnproven: But the Collatz conjecture has not been proven or disproven
chapter8.conclusionCollatz: Conclusion: undecidable (this is the Halting Problem)

appendix.title: Appendix: First Step to Machine Learning
appendix.coreConceptDesc: The perceptron is the simplest neural network and an excellent starting point for understanding machine learning. By adjusting weights, a perceptron can learn to classify data into different categories. This is the foundation of deep learning.
appendix.gradientDescentDesc: The perceptron adjusts weights through the gradient descent algorithm. Each time a prediction is wrong, the weights are nudged in the correct direction — like descending a mountain, gradually approaching the optimal solution.
appendix.keyInsightDesc: The perceptron is the "Hello World" of machine learning. It demonstrates how to automatically adjust parameters through data, letting programs "learn" to accomplish tasks. From perceptrons to deep neural networks, the core idea remains the same: optimize parameters through gradient descent so the model can fit data and make predictions.
appendix.perceptronDesc: Click the canvas to add blue or yellow data points, then click "Start Training" to watch the perceptron learn to classify.
appendix.forwardDesc: Compute weighted inputs, pass through activation function to get output
appendix.updateDesc: Adjust weights based on error to reduce next prediction error
appendix.neuralNetworkDesc: Multi-layer perceptrons form neural networks. The input layer receives data, hidden layers extract features, and the output layer gives results.

learningPath.subtitle: From theory to practice, 4 steps to master the programmer's mathematical mind
learningPath.step1Desc: Choose topics from 8 core chapters that interest you. Each chapter is an independent math thinking exercise.
learningPath.step2Desc: Through carefully written code examples, understand the practical application of math concepts in programs.
learningPath.step3Desc: Hands-on in the interactive lab. Deepen your understanding of math principles through visualization tools.
learningPath.step4Desc: Apply learned mathematical thinking to real programming problems and improve problem-solving skills.
learningPath.quote1: Mathematics is not about numbers, but about
